title: The peculiar response of Kelvin-Voigt chains with a free end
abstract: We exactly solve a model of a heterogeneous chain of overdamped, harmonically coupled particles with momentum-conserving dissipation. Despite being governed by a non-symmetric drift operator, the system admits an analytical diagonalization by use of a forward-difference transformation. In case of one free end, the response matrix shows a peculiar staircase form: the response of particle i to a force acting on particle j is independent of the properties and the length of the chain-part between i and j. For rank-deficient interaction matrices, the state space is decomposed into free and constrained subspaces. We demonstrate that this separation has clear physical consequences: the free subspace governs steady state responses, while the constraint subspace governs the relaxation after cessation of forcing. These results establish a framework for analyzing heterogeneous overdamped dynamics with momentum conservation.
summary: This paper provides an exact solution for the dynamics of heterogeneous, overdamped 1D chains with relative friction. It reveals a counter-intuitive 'X-ray vision' effect where the mechanical response is insensitive to intermediate chain properties and establishes how different subspaces of the system govern steady-state versus relaxation behaviors.
main_problem: The paper addresses the analytical treatment of heterogeneous, overdamped, one-dimensional harmonic chains with momentum-conserving dissipation, specifically focusing on the mechanical response when a free end is present.
main_result: The authors find that such chains exhibit an 'X-ray vision' property where the response of a particle to a force is independent of the material properties between them, and they demonstrate a decomposition of the state space into free and constrained subspaces governing steady-state and relaxation dynamics.
method: The study uses an exact analytical diagonalization of the drift operator via a forward-difference transformation to map site variables to bond variables.
model_or_system: A 1D Kelvin-Voigt chain consisting of $N$ harmonically coupled particles with nearest-neighbor stiffness and momentum-conserving (relative) friction, featuring one free end and one fixed end.
key_observables: The response matrix (susceptibility) $\chi_{ij}(t)$, steady-state velocity, and relaxation/recoil motion.
important_parameters: Overdamped limit, momentum-conserving dissipation, rank-deficient interaction matrices (free bonds), and the distinction between steady-state and relaxation regimes.
main_assumptions: The dynamics are assumed to be overdamped ($m 	o 0$) and the dissipation is strictly momentum-conserving (relative friction).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the staircase displacement profile in an undamped chain; Figure 2 illustrates the Kelvin-Voigt model; Figure 3 depicts the forward-difference operator $L$ and its inverse; Figure 4 visualizes the susceptibility matrix; Figure 7 shows a viscoelastic fluid between parallel plates.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of heterogeneous chains, defines the Kelvin-Voigt model and its mathematical framework, presents the analytical diagonalization method, derives the 'X-ray vision' response property, discusses the decomposition of state space into free and constrained subspaces, and applies the results to a viscoelastic fluid between plates.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Displacement profile in a massless undamped harmonic chain of length N, with left end free and the right end (site N + 1) fixed. Lower dots show the sites in absence of external forces, where xl = 0 for all l. Upper dots sketch the positions after application of an external force f acting on site j. This yields xl = xj for all l < j, implying a staircase form of the response matrix.

FIG. 2. Schematic diagram of the model in Eq. (1), consisting of N interacting DoFs with nearest-neighbor interaction κi, and relative friction γi. The left end is free while the right end is fixed, xN+1 = 0. The model thus represents a chain of Kelvin-Voigt units [23–25].

FIG. 3. Schematic for eigenmodes, exemplarily for N = 10. Left part of sketch demonstrates the eigenmode i = 2 via application of L, see Eq. (19). Right part of sketch illustrates action of L−1 to obtain x6 from eigenmodes, see Eq. (20).

FIG. 4. Schematic for the susceptibility χij in Eq. (26), quantifying the response at site i to a force at site j, exemplarily for N = 10. A force acting on j = 3 yields, via Eq. (16), a finite force Fl for l ≥3, so that these bonds show a response to the force. On the other hand, the response at site i = 7 is found from the bonds with l ≥7, see Eq. (20). Hence, the susceptibility χ73 = χ37 has contributions from bonds l ≥max{7, 3}, i.e., l ≥7.

FIG. 5. Susceptibility matrix, exemplary for N = 5. The color-code gives the number of bonds contributing to the respective element χij according to Eq. (26). Any element χij equals a diagonal entry, i.e., χij = χji = χll with l = max {i, j}.
